mnemonics: JVM's Primary Path: Every Java program starts with a main thread, like a main road leading into a program's city.
Main is the Boss: The main() method is the entry point where the boss thread starts its work and can delegate to worker threads.
Threads Have Lifecycles: Remember that the main thread follows the same lifecycle (New, Runnable, Running, Blocked, Dead) as any other thread.
keyPoints: The main thread is automatically created when a Java program starts and begins executing the main() method.
The main thread is the parent thread from which other 'child' threads can be spawned.
The main thread must be the last thread to finish execution because it often performs shutdown tasks; terminating it early can orphan child threads.
commonMistakes: Assuming the program ends when main() finishes, ignoring that child threads may still be running (non-daemon threads keep the JVM alive).
Directly calling the run() method instead of start() on a new thread, which executes the code in the main thread instead of a new one.
